topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my internal monologue. I watched Mark lean back in his chair, a picture of relaxed confidence as Sarah, the one who always seemed to glide through presentations, spoke about her projected impact. Every word she uttered felt polished, effortless. I fiddled with the edge of my notepad, the cheap paper rough beneath my fingers. My stomach tightened into a hard knot. I rehearsed a response in my head—something insightful, something that showcased my depth—but the words felt clumsy, tripping over themselves. When the meeting broke, I immediately started organizing my desk, stacking files with an almost frantic precision, trying to bury the churning uncertainty beneath a mountain of paperwork.

[story 2]

"That's a really solid strategy, Sarah," my manager commented, clapping her hands together. I managed a tight, practiced smile, forcing my gaze toward the water cooler instead of meeting anyone's eyes. Inside, a cold wave washed over me. I could practically feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a familiar flush of inadequacy. I focused intensely on the condensation dripping down my glass, tracing the rivulets with my fingertip. I wanted to interject with a supporting detail, a piece of data that would prove my worth, but the air felt thick, resistant. I just nodded, my throat suddenly dry, the silence stretching out until it felt unbearable.

[story 3]

I spent the entire lunch hour staring at my sandwich, the bread suddenly tasting like ash. Across the table, David was laughing easily with the VP, his gestures broad and open. I kept my hands folded neatly on the table, my posture rigid. Every time someone addressed me directly, a strange pressure built behind my eyes, making it difficult to maintain eye contact. I noticed the way his colleagues glanced my way—a quick, assessing sweep that made my shoulders hunch almost imperceptibly. I picked up my coffee mug, gripping it tightly enough that my knuckles turned white. I needed to appear engaged, competent, but all I could manage was a shallow, almost mechanical nod.

I watched Mark lean back in his chair, a picture of relaxed confidence as Sarah, the one who always seemed to glide through presentations, spoke about her projected impact. Every word she uttered felt polished, effortless. I fiddled with the edge of my notepad, the cheap paper rough beneath my fingers. My stomach tightened into a hard knot. When the meeting broke, I immediately started organizing my desk, stacking files with an almost frantic precision, trying to bury the churning uncertainty beneath a mountain of paperwork.

I managed a tight, practiced smile, forcing my gaze toward the water cooler instead of meeting anyone's eyes. Inside, a cold wave washed over me. I could practically feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a familiar flush of inadequacy. I focused intensely on the condensation dripping down my glass, tracing the rivulets with my fingertip. I spent the entire lunch hour staring at my sandwich, the bread suddenly tasting like ash. Across the table, David was laughing easily with the VP, his gestures broad and open. I kept my hands folded neatly on the table, my posture rigid. Every time someone addressed me directly, a strange pressure built behind my eyes, making it difficult to maintain eye contact. I picked up my coffee mug, gripping it tightly enough that my knuckles turned white. I needed to appear engaged, competent, but all I could manage was a shallow, almost mechanical nod.
